provenance.note: Every signature-guidance line on the page carries one of these source ids. Stripe facts are the SWTK material already committed in this repo; GitHub facts were verified against the official docs on the fetch date; Discord's interaction-object body fields were verified against the official docs, but the signature-verification specifics were NOT on the fetched page, so that guidance is honestly downgraded to a bare pointer at the official docs.

sources.label: SWTK material via botsite/data/stripe_gotchas.json @ venture-lab 0679327
sources.detail: menno420/venture-lab @ 0679327a463c063dcd9fc62b33ffb9a3789fa7d3, curated into this repo as botsite/data/stripe_gotchas.json (retrieved 2026-07-13)

sources.label: GitHub Docs — Validating webhook deliveries (fetched 2026-07-13)
sources.detail: Verified from source this session: X-Hub-Signature-256 header, HMAC-SHA256 hex digest of the payload keyed by the webhook secret, sha256= prefix, constant-time comparison.

sources.label: GitHub Docs — Webhook events and payloads (fetched 2026-07-13)
sources.detail: Verified from source this session: common body properties action, sender, repository (plus organization / installation when applicable).

sources.label: Discord Docs — Receiving and responding (fetched 2026-07-13; body fields only)
sources.detail: Verified from source this session: the interaction object carries id, application_id, type, token (and data). The signature-verification specifics were NOT on the fetched page, so Discord signature guidance is downgraded to a pointer at the official docs — nothing invented.

sources.label: Discord signature scheme — not verified from source this session; see the official docs
sources.detail: The page fetched this session documented the interaction object but not the request-signing scheme. Rather than repeat signature specifics from memory, this analyzer only points at the official docs for them.

markers.label: top-level type is a string (e.g. checkout.session.completed)

lines.text: The Stripe-Signature value is HMAC-SHA256 over "{t}." + raw_body, keyed by your whsec_ secret — recompute it and constant-time compare against each v1=, rejecting on mismatch.
Enforce the 300-second timestamp tolerance on t= — without it, a captured request can be replayed indefinitely.
Verify over the RAW request bytes: re-serialising the JSON (different key order or whitespace) changes the bytes and breaks the signature.
Return 2xx fast and process idempotently — Stripe retries on any non-2xx, so dedupe on event.id and do heavy work out-of-band.

field_notes.text: This payload has data.object.customer_email = null — that is NORMAL for checkout.session.completed. The buyer's address is at customer_details.email; reading only customer_email silently drops the sale.
Stripe's success_url expands only {CHECKOUT_SESSION_ID} — any other placeholder is passed through literally.

markers.label: action + repository + sender (the common event-payload shape)

lines.text: GitHub sends the signature in the X-Hub-Signature-256 header: an HMAC-SHA256 hex digest of the payload, keyed by your webhook secret, always prefixed sha256=.
Compare with a constant-time method — GitHub's docs say never use a plain == comparison, to mitigate timing attacks.

Discord signs interaction requests, but the signature-verification specifics were not verified from source this session — see the official docs: https://discord.com/developers/docs/interactions/receiving-and-responding

generic.header_reminder: Signature headers ride the HTTP request, not the JSON body — a pasted payload alone can never be signature-verified. This tool reads shapes; verification needs the raw request bytes plus its headers.

heuristics.label: unix epoch seconds/millis whose value falls in 2001–2286 decode to an ISO date and are labeled "epoch timestamp (inferred)" — a plausible-range guess, not a certainty
ISO-8601-shaped strings are labeled as such
Stripe-style prefixed ids (evt_, cus_, ch_, pi_, sub_, cs_, in_, prod_, price_) are labeled with which object they name
UUID-shaped strings are labeled as such
long numeric strings (17+ digits) are labeled "possible snowflake id (unverified)"
email-shaped strings and http(s) URLs are labeled by shape
everything else is honestly "unknown" — no classification is invented

generic.limits: The field walk is capped at 6 levels deep and 500 fields; the results say so when truncated.
